abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a generalist robotic agent. However, existing VLAs are hindered by excessive parameter scales, prohibitive pre-training requirements, and limited applicability to diverse embodiments. To improve the practicality of VLAs, we propose a comprehensive benchmark and an improved baseline. First, we propose CEBench, a new benchmark spanning diverse embodiments in both simulation and the real world with consideration of domain randomization. We collect 14.4k simulated trajectories and 1.6k real-world expert-curated trajectories to support training on CEBench. Second, using CEBench as our testbed, we study three critical aspects of VLAs' practicality and offer several key findings. Informed by these findings, we introduce LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight yet powerful VLA designed for practical deployment on consumer-grade GPUs. Architecturally, it integrates a compact VLM backbone with multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, and action chunking. To eliminate reliance on costly pre-training, LLaVA-VLA adopts a two-stage training paradigm including post-training and fine-tuning. Furthermore, LLaVA-VLA extends the action space to unify navigation and manipulation. Experiments across embodiments demonstrate the capabilities of generalization and versatility of LLaVA-VLA , while real-world mobile manipulation experiments establish it as the first end-to-end VLA model for mobile manipulation. We will open-source all datasets, codes, and checkpoints upon acceptance to foster reproducibility and future research.

keyInsights: Model performance is not strictly proportional to parameter scale; lightweight designs like LLaVA-VLA can achieve comparable or superior results to larger, pre-trained models through intelligent architectural choices.

Extensive cross-embodiment large-scale pre-training is not essential; effective vision-to-action mapping can be established through post-training on in-domain, multi-task data, followed by task-specific fine-tuning.
A unified action space using discrete direction and value tokens allows for stable and flexible switching between navigation and manipulation modalities, enabling complex mobile manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from excessive parameter scales, making them computationally intensive and difficult to deploy on resource-constrained platforms.
Current VLA models require prohibitive pre-training on vast robotic datasets, leading to high computational costs and limiting accessibility.
Most VLAs demonstrate limited applicability, primarily in fixed-base manipulation settings, lacking versatility for cross-embodiment tasks such as mobile manipulation.
results: LLaVA-VLA (0.5B parameters) achieved competitive or superior performance on single-arm (CALVIN) and bimanual (RoboTwin) manipulation tasks compared to much larger (3B, 7B) and pre-trained models.
The model demonstrated robust performance in real-world fixed-base bimanual manipulation, maintaining higher success rates under domain randomization than baselines (ACT, TinyVLA).
LLaVA-VLA became the first end-to-end VLA model to successfully handle real-world mobile manipulation tasks, outperforming baselines in tasks requiring both navigation and precise manipulation.
solution: A comprehensive benchmark, CEBench, was proposed to evaluate VLA models across diverse robotic embodiments (single-arm, bimanual, mobile bimanual) in both simulated and real-world scenarios, incorporating explicit domain randomization.
LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight (0.5B parameters) and pre-training-free VLA model, was developed using a compact LLaVA-OneVision backbone and a two-stage training approach.
LLaVA-VLA integrates multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, action chunking, and a unified hybrid action space designed for seamless navigation and manipulation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou) developed CEBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models across diverse robotic embodiments and domain randomization. They also introduced LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight, pre-training-free baseline that achieves competitive performance on various manipulation tasks, including being the first end-to-end VLA model capable of mobile manipulation.

keyInsights: VLA performance is not strictly proportional to parameter scale; lightweight models (0.5B) can achieve or surpass the performance of much larger (3B/7B) models.

A two-stage training paradigm (post-training on in-domain multi-task data followed by fine-tuning) is sufficient for small models, eliminating the need for costly large-scale cross-embodiment pre-training.

Effective VLA design for practical scenarios benefits from vertically concatenated multi-view images, proprioceptive tokenization, action chunking, and a unified discrete action space with appropriate granularity.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are prohibitively large, with billions of parameters, requiring immense computational resources for training and inference.
Developing VLAs typically necessitates costly and extensive pre-training on vast, multi-modal, and multi-task robotic datasets.
Existing VLA models primarily focus on fixed-base manipulation, lacking versatility for diverse embodiments and complex mobile manipulation scenarios.
results: LLaVA-VLA (0.5B) outperformed larger VLA models (e.g., 3B RoboFlamingo, 7B OpenVLA) on single-arm manipulation tasks (CALVIN), achieving 94.8% success on the first sub-task.
Demonstrated superior visual generalization on bimanual manipulation under domain randomization (RoboTwin), with a 28.6% average success rate, significantly outperforming baselines like RDT (11.4%).

Successfully performed real-world mobile manipulation tasks like 'Move and fetch bottles' and 'Move and open the drawer' with 4/10 success rates, making it the first end-to-end VLA model to handle such scenarios.
solution: Developed CEBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating VLA practicality across diverse robotic embodiments, including single-arm, bimanual, and real-world bimanual mobile manipulation, incorporating domain randomization.
Introduced LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight (0.5B parameters), pretraining-free VLA model built upon a compact LLaVA-OneVision VLM backbone.
Designed a hybrid action space for LLaVA-VLA that unifies navigation and manipulation actions, enabling seamless transitions and control for mobile manipulation tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed a comprehensive benchmark (CEBench) and an improved baseline model, LLaVA-VLA, to enhance the practicality of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. LLaVA-VLA is a lightweight, pretraining-free model capable of real-world mobile manipulation, demonstrating performance comparable to or exceeding larger models across diverse robotic tasks.
